intentional_test_features: Age-anchoring trap: peripheral arterial disease is classically a disease of older patients with atherosclerosis — AI Doctor must recognise that a young heavy smoker with distal-only ischaemia and preserved proximal pulses points to a different (non-atherosclerotic) vascular process, not simply 'too young for PAD so probably not vascular'
Smoking as causative agent, not just risk factor — the case tests whether the AI Doctor identifies tobacco cessation as an urgent, disease-modifying necessity rather than routine lifestyle advice
Time-critical tissue viability: black/necrotic toe plus rest pain plus absent distal pulses is a limb-threatening emergency that must not be managed as routine claudication

Distracting alternative framing: patient may focus on pain relief and analgesia rather than volunteering the smoking history or the color change — AI Doctor must actively elicit smoking history and screen for tissue colour change
Under-triage risk: a young, otherwise well-looking patient describing 'foot pain' could be trivialised without active screening for ischaemic features

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
